
<h3>Installing required software</h3>
The only prerequisite for the R training is to install the latest version of R and RStudio on your computer. These should be available in the Company Portal in Entra, and shouldn't require special permissions to install. We'll talk about the difference between R and RStudio on the first day, but for now, just make sure they're installed. 

Your R version should be at least 4.4.3 or above to make sure everyone's code behaves the same way. Likely the R version you have is 4.5.2, and the RStudio version is 2025.06.2-418 or higher. This is how it may appear in Company Portal. If your machine hasn't migrated to Entra yet, it may appear different.

<center><img src = "./images/R_in_company_portal.png" alt = 'R in company portal' style = "width:500px;"></center>
</br>
<details open><summary class='drop'><b style="color:red">Required Packages</b></summary>
A number of packages are required to follow along with data wrangling and visualization sessions. Please try to install these in RStudio ahead of time by running the code below. If you don't know how to run the code, open view the <b>Running Code Screencast</b> below for how to do this. 
```{r reqpkg, echo = T, eval = F}
packages <- c("tidyverse", # for Day 2 and 3 data wrangling
              "RColorBrewer", "viridis", "patchwork", # for Day 3 ggplot
              "readxl", "writexl") # for day 1 importing from excel

install.packages(setdiff(packages, rownames(installed.packages())))  

# Check that installation worked
library(tidyverse) # turns on core tidyverse packages
library(RColorBrewer) # palette generator
library(viridis) # more palettes
library(patchwork) # multipanel plots
library(readxl) # reading xlsx
library(writexl) # writing xlsx
```

<details><summary class='drop2'>Running Code Screencast</summary>
<video height = "650" controls>
<source src="images/Installing_packages.mp4" type="video/mp4">
</video>
</details>
</details>
<br>

<details open><summary class='drop'>Optional Reading</summary>
This is completely optional, but if you have any time before training starts, I highly recommend reading <a href="https://stat545.com/r-basics.html">Chapter 2: R basics and workflows</a> in STAT545. This is an online book based on a graduate level statistics class that Dr. Jenny Bryan teaches and is turning into this book. She's a stats professor turned senior programmer at RStudio, and I really like how she approaches programming R in this book. 
</details>
<br>

<details open><summary class='drop'>About the Training</summary>
<ul>
<li><b>Timing:</b> The training will take place over 3 half days. Each day will run from 9am - 1pm EST via MS Teams. </li>

<li><b>Office Hours:</b> For the day prior to training and each afternoon following training, I will be available from 1 - 3pm EST for office hours in case there are questions that couldn't be handled before or during training. I will send a Teams link to participants for this office hours. Teams chats are also fine for posing questions. </li>

<li><b>Structure:</b> For most of the training, I will share my screen as I go through the website and then demo with live coding. Having 2 screens, one for my screen share and one for your R session, will make following along a lot easier. </li>

<li><b>Getting Help:</b> I intentionally included people in this training I know to be kind, capable, and that will benefit from having better R skills. My hope is that this group is small and supportive enough that everyone feels comfortable openly asking questions and providing feedback to the group. However, if you aren't comfortable asking questions openly, you can ask questions in the anonymous <a href="https://forms.office.com/g/vY0KbqxAXr">training feedback form</a>. Additionally, if someone runs into an issue I can't immediately troubleshoot (it happens), we may have to table it until office hours. We will then discuss it with the group the next day (if relevant). </li>

<li><b>Objectives:</b> Three days is barely enough to scratch the surface on what you can do in R. My goals with this training are to:
<ol>
<li>Help you get beyond the initial learning curve that can be really challenging to climb on your own.</li>
<li>Expose you to what I consider are the useful things R can do for us. </li>
<li>Provide you the tools needed to continue advancing your R skills on your own.</li>
</ol>
</li>

<li><b>Credit:</b> Much of this training was borrowed heavily from <a href = "https://katemmiller.github.io/IMD_R_Training_Intro/">IMD Intro to R</a> training in 2022. A ton of credit for this training goes to the developers of those lessons:
<ul>
<li>Day 1 Intro to R: Sarah Wright and Andrew Birch</li>
<li>Day 2 Data Wrangling: John Paul Schmit and Lauren Pandori</li>
<li>Day 3 Data Visualization: Ellen Cheng and Kate Miller (Spatial Data) </li>
<li>Day 4 Programming Best Practices: Sarah Wright and Thomas Parr </li>
</ul>
</li>

<li><b>Feedback:</b> Finally, to help me improve this training for future sessions, please leave feedback in the <a href="https://forms.office.com/g/vY0KbqxAXr">training feedback form</a>. You can submit feedback multiple times and don't need to answer every question. Responses are anonymous.  </li>
</ul>
</details>
<br>
